sentence: Subjective:
HPI: Mr. Watson is an 86-year-old male who returns in follow-up for adult hydrocephalus.
He underwent shunt surgery on August 1st, 2020, and has been experiencing progressive gait impairment, cognitive impairment, and decreased bladder control for eight to ten years.
He has been doing better since the surgery, with improved walking and bladder control, but still requires assistance and wears diapers.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of adult hydrocephalus, for which he underwent shunt surgery on August 1st, 2020.
He has also had a Medtronic Strata programmable shunt in the ventricular peritoneal configuration placed, which is currently set at a level of 2.0.
Review of systems: The patient has had progressive problems affecting his walking, thinking, and bladder control, which have improved since surgery.
He still experiences occasional difficulty walking and requires assistance, and has urinary incontinence.
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's blood pressure is 124/80, heart rate is 64, respirations are 18, weight is 174 pounds, and pain is zero out of 10.
Physical exam: The shunt site was clean, dry, and intact with a confirmed setting of 2.0.

The patient was tested for recent and remote memory, attention span, and concentration in fund of knowledge, and scored a 26 out of 30 on the MMSE when tested with spelling and 25 out of 30 when tested with calculations.
He was able to get two of the three memory words with cuing, and the third with multiple choice.

Gait testing using the Tinetti assessment tool showed a gait score of 6 to 8 out of 12 and a balance score of 12 out of 16 for a total score of 18 to 20 out of 28, which is slightly improved from his initial score.
Cranial nerves, pupils are equal, EOMs are intact, face is symmetric, and no disarchria.
Motor is normal for bulk and strength, coordination is slow from no- finger to nose.
The CT scan from the head of 10/15/2020 shows a frontal horn span at the level of foramen of Monro of 4.6 centimeters with a third ventricular contour that is flat with a span of 10 millimeters.
There is a tiny amount of blood in the right front region with a tiny subdermal collection, which was not noticed or noted by the radiologist who stated any extra-axial fluid collections.
Test Results: [NOT MENTIONED]
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has made significant improvements in his walking and bladder control since shunt surgery, but still requires assistance and has ongoing cognitive impairment.
Problem list: Adult hydrocephalus; Gait impairment; Urinary incontinence and urgency; Cognitive impairment; Status: confirmed.
Plan:
I will monitor the patient's condition and adjust the shunt setting as needed.
I will see the patient again in three months to re-evaluate his condition and consider adjusting the shunt setting or referring him to a driver safety program.
Follow-up: The patient will be seen again in three months to re-evaluate his condition and consider adjusting the shunt setting or referring him to a driver safety program.
